lens.included_activities: Privately controlled U.S. multi-person sports-training, coaching, scouting, athlete-performance, and competition-support firms whose recurring external service prepares or supports participants in spectator sports events or competitions.
lens.excluded_activities: Independent athletes whose revenue depends on their own performance, owners of cars, horses, dogs, or other racing participants, single-person practices without transferable operations, sports instruction unconnected to spectator competition, agents and career managers, captive team departments, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat outsourced support sold to athletes, families, teams, schools, clubs, and competition organizations through monthly memberships, session packages, season or team contracts, retainers, assessments, and specialized project fees. Delivery combines recurring coaching or training with performance analysis, program design, documentation, travel, and event support.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code combines independent athletes, owners of racing participants, and establishments supplying specialized support to sports participants. Their labor, transferability, and revenue models are incompatible, so the lens is narrowed to multi-person sports-training and competition-support service firms for coherence, without selecting them for attractiveness.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader four-digit Spectator Sports occupation mix, not the narrowed six-digit service-firm lens, while O*NET describes tasks rather than measuring current AI exposure. I mapped video and performance review, opponent and athlete research, initial program drafting, records, reports, scheduling, customer communication, marketing, and administration to partial exposure; direct instruction, motivation, emergency response, physical assessment, hands-on care, and event presence receive little substitution weight. The broader BLS mix includes highly paid athletes and team personnel, which can bias the wage-weighted result in either direction relative to specialist support firms.
a.rationale: BLS reports coaches and scouts at 7.88% of broader Spectator Sports employment, management at 9.60%, sales at 8.13%, and office and administrative support at 8.17%. O*NET shows coaches analyze opponents, evaluate athletes, review performance records, coordinate travel, communicate with families, plan training, and directly instruct and motivate. Athletic trainers assess injuries, report progress, keep records, write reports, deliver care, provide clearances, and collaborate with physicians. The information-heavy portions are exposed, but much of the value is embodied, interpersonal, safety-sensitive, or dependent on live observation.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit payroll-by-occupation extract for 711219, filtered to multi-person support firms, plus task-level time studies and wage weights from at least fifteen firms and an inventory of already deployed video, scheduling, CRM, documentation, and program-design automation would materially change the estimate.
caveats: There is no six-digit occupation table for the narrowed service-firm population.
O*NET establishes task content but not time shares, wage weights, technical exposure, or the current automation baseline.
The frozen compensation ratio includes independent athletes and racing-participant businesses whose compensation economics may differ sharply from support firms.

rho.rationale: Smaller support firms can adopt packaged tools for video tagging, research, scheduling, billing, CRM, communication, reports, and draft programs without building software. Implementation should be slower for recommendations tied to injury, return-to-play, athlete selection, or competition strategy because quality depends on context, trust, and accountable review. Nearly all states require athletic trainers to be licensed or certified, and BLS describes emergency care and physician-directed work, which makes human-in-the-loop deployment the practical ceiling for clinical tasks.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor telemetry and two-season before-and-after operating data from coaching, scouting, athlete-management, and athletic-training platforms, including adoption, exception rates, labor hours removed, client retention, injury incidents, and professional-liability claims, would materially move this estimate.
caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than an observed implementation rate.
Fragmented software, inconsistent video and sensor data, client consent, and professional-liability concerns may slow adoption.
Implementation will differ materially between scouting analysis, strength coaching, clinical athletic training, race-animal support, and event logistics.

e.rationale: Census defines 711219 as three distinct populations: independent athletes, owners of racing participants, and specialized sports-support establishments. Only the last population fits the recurring outsourced-service lens, and it must also have multi-person operations, institutionalized client relationships, and transferable control. No current six-digit source separates receipts or firms across those populations, so eligibility is bounded well below the whole-code count.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level 711219 census cross-walked to Census activity, employee count, service revenue, customer concentration, owner dependence, racing-participant ownership, EBITDA band, and transferable legal entity would materially change eligibility.
caveats: The frozen firm count covers the full code, while this lens excludes independent athletes and racing-participant owners.
High-earning personal athletes can enter the frozen economic band without creating a transferable service business.
Some apparent sports-performance firms may instead be classified as sports instruction, fitness centers, or healthcare practices.

s5.rationale: Broad ownership evidence shows that more than half of U.S. business owners were at least age 55 in the 2019 Annual Business Survey, and BizBuySell reported 9,546 broad small-business transactions in 2024, so succession and a functioning sale channel are real. Neither source covers sports-support firms. Transferability is constrained because reputation, athlete trust, methods, and customer relationships often reside with a founder or star coach; the estimate counts only control transfers of an intact operating firm and excludes departures, acqui-hires without continuity, and internal reorganizations.
s5.change_evidence: State corporate histories, PitchBook or Capital IQ transactions, business-broker listings, and interviews with owners of scaled sports-performance and scouting firms should be combined with owner age, succession plan, client concentration, staff tenure, and founder-delivery hours over the last ten years.
caveats: Owner-age evidence is broad, dated, and does not imply a sale or a five-year event probability.
BizBuySell data are voluntarily reported and do not identify 711219 or lower-middle-market transactions.
Personal goodwill can disappear at retirement even when a legal entity changes hands.

q.bridge: UCLA Health publishes fixed membership prices for sport-specific performance and return-to-play training, not realized retention of AI benefits by 711219 firms. I use the monthly and longer-duration fixed-price structure to infer little automatic contractual pass-through but substantial renewal repricing and competitive sharing. A health-system program may have stronger brand and broader bundled services than an independent firm, which can overstate pricing power and retention.
q.rationale: UCLA lists fixed memberships for twelve monthly sessions at $1,149 for one month, $1,049 for three months, and $999 for twelve months; its five-session option similarly declines from $749 to $599 with term. This structure supports retention of within-term administrative and delivery savings, but also shows price sensitivity and explicit sharing for commitment. Over five years, frequent renewals, transparent alternatives, coach portability, client churn, and competitive reinvestment should leak a meaningful share of gross benefit even without cost-plus billing.
q.change_evidence: Firm-level cohorts linking implemented labor savings to renewal prices, discounts, utilization, coach pay, client churn, gross margin, and contract wins across individual memberships, team contracts, school outsourcing, and scouting retainers would materially change retention.
caveats: The pricing source is one branded program and may fall in an adjacent healthcare or fitness classification.
Institutional team and school contracts may be bid, hourly, or labor-cost-linked rather than membership-priced.
Retention differs between scalable analysis and administration versus coach time saved in a high-touch membership.

d5.bridge: BLS projects occupation employment, NCAA reports college participation, and SFIA measures broad activity participation; none directly forecasts constant-price demand for 711219 outsourced support services. I translate the midpoint of the 2024-to-2034 athletic-trainer and coach or scout employment outlooks into a five-year service-demand case, using participation growth as a directional cross-check. Occupational demand includes schools, hospitals, teams, and self-employment outside the lens, which may overstate addressable outsourced demand; outsourcing penetration and premium performance spending can push the other way.
d5.rationale: BLS projects athletic-trainer employment to grow 11% and coach or scout employment to grow 6% from 2024 to 2034. NCAA reports 554,298 championship-sport athletes in the 2024 academic year ending in 2025, up 15,368 in one year, while SFIA reports 250 million active Americans in 2025 and 1.2% annual participation growth. These indicators support modest five-year growth in training, scouting, performance, and competition support, tempered by school budgets, household affordability, sport-specific volatility, and possible substitution by team staff or digital products.
d5.change_evidence: A quarterly U.S. panel of 711219 support-firm bookings, active athletes, team and school contracts, sessions, real price, churn, utilization, and customer budgets by sport and service type, together with procurement awards and participation forecasts, would materially change demand.
caveats: Employment growth is not a constant-quality service-volume measure and can reflect insourcing rather than outsourced demand.
NCAA athletes and broad recreation participants are larger populations than paying customers for the narrowed professional-support lens.
Aggregate growth can conceal contraction in individual sports, racing support, geographies, or customer income segments.

o.bridge: O*NET and BLS measure duties, regulation, and employment rather than the future operator-required share. I classify direct instruction, motivation, observation, physical assessment, emergency response, licensed care, relationship management, and accountable program adjustment as continuing operator work, while allowing research, generic plans, video tagging, records, scheduling, and some remote coaching to become self-service or software-supplied. The clinical athletic-trainer evidence can bias operator persistence upward relative to scouting and nonclinical coaching.
o.rationale: Coaches must conduct practice, adjust technique, motivate, instruct movement, enforce safety, and evaluate athletes in context. Athletic trainers provide emergency care, assess injuries, deliver rehabilitation, issue readiness clearances, and usually require licensing or certification. Software can supply generic programming, analytics, and asynchronous feedback, and some customers will self-serve, but competitive participants still need a firm accountable for individualized delivery, safety, coordination, and outcomes.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal client and staffing data from firms offering in-person, hybrid, and software-only products, with retention, outcomes, incident rates, willingness to pay, and the share of services legally requiring a credentialed human, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: Operator requirement varies substantially between physical athletic care, live coaching, video scouting, and generic programming.
Remote delivery can still require an accountable operator even when it removes local labor and facility use.
The estimate concerns service quantity requiring a lens-type operator, not preservation of current jobs or delivery channels.

narrative.executive_view: The usable opportunity is the specialized service-firm slice of a highly heterogeneous code, not independent athletes or racing-participant owners. These firms have a meaningful information and administration layer that AI can compress, while their direct coaching, physical evaluation, motivation, emergency response, and trusted judgment remain difficult to remove. Participation and occupation forecasts support modest demand growth, but the eligible firm count and control-transfer history are not directly observed and personal goodwill is a central acquisition risk.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate performance-video review, opponent and recruit research, individualized plan drafts, session notes, progress reports, scheduling, billing, CRM, marketing, and routine client communication. It can also standardize knowledge across coaches and reduce avoidable administrative hiring. Humans remain responsible for live observation, technique correction, relationship and motivation, clinical assessment, emergency care, safety, complex program adjustment, and final selection or return-to-play decisions.
narrative.value_capture: Monthly memberships, packages, and seasonal contracts give operators a window to retain productivity improvements because invoices are generally fixed rather than cost-plus. The protection is incomplete: renewal cycles are frequent, prices are transparent, clients can follow a coach, and firms may share savings through discounts or add service to retain athletes. Institutional contracts may expose savings more quickly through rebids.
narrative.firm_availability: Census combines three economically different populations in 711219. Independent athletes and owners of racing participants can have substantial receipts but do not represent recurring outsourced service firms, so the frozen whole-code count materially exceeds the coherent pool. Among support firms, only businesses with a team, repeat customers, documented methods, and relationships that survive the founder qualify as transferable operations.
narrative.demand_durability: Athletic-trainer and coach or scout employment projections are positive, NCAA participation reached a record 554,298 championship-sport athletes, and broad activity participation also rose. These are proxies rather than firm revenue, but they support modest growth in the need for athlete preparation and support. Software-only plans and team insourcing can take some quantity; the remaining individualized, safety-sensitive, and live-delivery demand still requires an accountable operator.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The six-digit code lacks a clean occupation mix, service-firm share, transfer history, and pricing panel. Classification leakage into sports instruction, fitness centers, healthcare, and agents is substantial. The frozen compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and includes personal-performance businesses, while the frozen firm count is an estimate across the entire code. A bad case is a founder-led company whose clients and coaches leave after sale while generic training and analysis migrate to software.
narrative.principal_driver: AI can scale the analysis, documentation, communication, and administration surrounding high-value human coaching and athlete support while fixed-price recurring revenue preserves part of the gain.
narrative.principal_weakness: The eligible population is only an unmeasured subset of a mixed code, and founder reputation and client trust can make apparent firms nontransferable.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: 711219 Other Spectator Sports
sources.supports: Defines the code's three populations: independent athletes, owners of racing participants, and establishments such as sports trainers providing specialized support for sports events or competitions.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: Spectator Sports
sources.supports: Provides broader-industry occupation shares, including coaches and scouts at 7.88%, management at 9.60%, sales at 8.13%, and office and administrative support at 8.17%.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Athletic Trainers
sources.supports: Documents injury assessment, progress reporting, direct care, readiness clearances, records, reports, instruction, rehabilitation, travel, prevention programs, and physical-field inspection tasks.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Coaches and Scouts
sources.supports: Documents practice planning, instruction, motivation, performance analysis, athlete evaluation, record review, travel coordination, safety enforcement, and customer communication.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Athletic Trainers
sources.supports: Reports 33,900 jobs in 2024, projected 11% employment growth by 2034, licensing or certification in nearly all states, and duties spanning emergency care, rehabilitation, prevention, records, and reports.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Coaches and Scouts
sources.supports: Reports 306,500 jobs in 2024, projected 6% growth by 2034, and duties including analysis, practice, instruction, motivation, records, recruitment, and direct athlete interaction.

sources.title: NCAA: A Record Number of Student-Athletes Participated in the 2024 Academic Year Ending in 2025
sources.supports: Reports 554,298 championship-sport athletes, an annual increase of 15,368, and 19,928 sponsored teams.

sources.title: Sports and Fitness Industry Association 2026 Topline Participation Findings
sources.supports: Reports 250 million U.S. sport, fitness, or leisure participants in 2025, 1.2% annual overall growth, and team sports as the strongest-growth category.

sources.title: UCLA Health Sports Performance: One-to-One Personal Training
sources.supports: Shows sport-specific, return-to-play, individualized training sold through fixed memberships, with twelve monthly sessions priced at $1,149, $1,049, or $999 depending on term.

sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau Annual Business Survey Visualizations
sources.supports: States that more than half of U.S. business owners were age 55 or older in the 2019 Annual Business Survey; used only as broad succession context.

sources.title: BizBuySell 2024 Insight Report
sources.supports: Reports 9,546 voluntarily reported small-business transactions in 2024 and identifies service businesses as 40% of transactions; used only as broad transfer-channel evidence.
